full_response:
Let  

 G=(V ,E ,w) be a connected, undirected graph, |V| = n, |E| ≥ n, all edge-weights distinct.  
 T₁  ⊂ E is the (unique) minimum-spanning tree (MST) of G,  
 W₁ = w(T₁) its weight.

Goal Find a spanning tree T₂≠T₁ of minimum weight  
   (“second–best MST”).

We give an O(n²) algorithm and prove its correctness.

────────────────────────────────────────
1 Structure of a second–best MST
────────────────────────────────────────
Lemma 1 If all edge weights are distinct, every spanning tree T≠T₁ that is
closest in weight to T₁ differs from T₁ in exactly one edge.

Proof (by exchange argument)  
Let T be any spanning tree ≠ T₁.  Traverse the (ordered) list of edges of T
and T₁; let e be the first edge that appears in T but not in T₁.  
Adding e to T₁ creates a unique cycle C.  Because
weights are distinct, the heaviest edge on C is unique; call it f.
By the cut property f∉T₁ has larger weight than every edge of C\{f},
hence f≠e and w(f) > w(e).  
Form T′ := T₁ − {f} ∪ {e}.  Then

 w(T′) = W₁ + w(e) − w(f) < W₁.

T′ differs from T₁ in the single edge e.  Repeating this
operation for T yields a tree differing by exactly one edge and having weight
≤ w(T).  Therefore the optimum
second-best tree can be obtained by exchanging one edge. ∎



Lemma 2 For every non-tree edge e=(u,v) let
 M(u,v) = heaviest-edge weight on the u–v path in T₁.  
Let

 Δ(e) = w(e) − M(u,v)  (>0 by the cut property),

and let ê minimise Δ(e).  
Then T₂ = T₁ − {ĥ} ∪ {ê},  where ĥ is the edge that attains M(û,v̂),
is the unique second-best MST and

 w(T₂) = W₁ + Δ(ê).

Proof.  
Replacing the heaviest edge on the T₁-path by e forms a spanning tree of
weight W₁+Δ(e).  By Lemma 1 the second-best tree must have this
form, hence choosing ê that minimises Δ(e) gives the minimum weight > W₁.  
Weights are distinct, so the minimum is unique. ∎



Thus the task reduces to

 For every non-tree edge (u,v) compute M(u,v),  
 take the minimum of W₁ + w(u,v) − M(u,v).

────────────────────────────────────────
2 Computing all M(u,v) in O(n²)
────────────────────────────────────────
T₁ is a tree on n vertices, so it has n−1 edges.

Fix a vertex s.  A depth-first (or breadth-first) traversal of T₁ from s
records, for every vertex x,  

 maxEdge(s,x) = heaviest edge on the s–x path.

Because T₁ has only n−1 edges, such a traversal costs O(n).
Repeating this for every start vertex yields the full n×n
table M(u,v) in time

 Σ_{s∈V} O(n) = O(n²).

Space: an n×n array (Θ(n²)).

────────────────────────────────────────
3 Complete algorithm
────────────────────────────────────────
We implement Prim’s MST algorithm with an adjacency-matrix representation;
its running time is O(n²) and therefore fits our budget.

Pseudocode
-----------------------------------------------------------------
SecondBestMST(G)
1  T₁ , W₁ ← PrimMST(G)              ▹ O(n²)
2  build adjacency list of the tree T₁
3  for every vertex s∈V               ▹ n traversals
4        DFS(s) on T₁ keeping current maximum edge
5        store result in table M[·][·]      ▹ total O(n²)
6  best2 ← +∞ ,  (ê, ĥ) ← (nil,nil)
7  for each edge e=(u,v)∈E\T₁         ▹ O(|E|) ≤ O(n²)
8        cand ← W₁ + w(e) − M[u][v]
9        if cand < best2
10             best2 ← cand
11             ê ← e ; ĥ ← “edge on u–v path whose weight = M[u][v]”
12 return   T₂ = T₁ − {ĥ} ∪ {ê}   ,  weight = best2
-----------------------------------------------------------------

────────────────────────────────────────
4 Complexity analysis
────────────────────────────────────────
Step 1 Prim with matrix          O(n²) time, O(n²) space  
Step 3–5 all-pairs M(u,v)       O(n²) time, O(n²) space  
Step 7–11 scan non-tree edges   O(|E|) ≤ O(n²) time, O(1) extra space  

Total     O(n²) time, Θ(n²) space.

────────────────────────────────────────
5 Correctness proof
────────────────────────────────────────
The algorithm outputs tree T₂ = T₁ − {ĥ} ∪ {ê}.  
By construction T₂ is a spanning tree.

By Lemma 2 its weight is W₁ + Δ(ê) and is the smallest strictly larger
than W₁ among all spanning trees, hence T₂ is the (unique) second-best MST. ∎



────────────────────────────────────────
6 Result
────────────────────────────────────────
\boxed{A\;second\text{-}best\;MST\;can\;be\;found\;in\;O(|V|^{2})\;time\;and\;Θ(|V|^{2})\;space\;
by\; (1)\;building\;the\;MST, (2)\;pre-computing\;for\;all\;pairs\;
the\;heaviest\;tree-edge\;on\;their\;MST\;path, and (3)\;choosing\;
the\;non-tree\;edge\;whose\;replacement\;produces\;the\;smallest\;weight\;increase.}

Confidence: 0.26
